title: Graphs: BFS (Java / Python)
general_notes: No double deduction (except in point 5). Do not deduct again for an error already penalised in an earlier point. For example: if a student uses LIFO instead of FIFO, base the overall correctness marks on whether the traversal is otherwise consistent with their dequeue strategy.
This rubric applies to both Java and Python submissions. The Java method is bfs(int[][] graph, int start); the Python function is bfs(graph, start). Both are correct.
The graph is represented as an adjacency list (array of arrays). Nodes are numbered from 0. The graph is directed and unweighted.
The output must list nodes in the order they are first visited (processed) by BFS. Nodes within each level are visited in the order they appear in the adjacency list.

rubric_points.name: BFS loop structure and initialisation
rubric_points.target: The start node is added to the queue before the loop, and a while loop processes nodes until the queue is empty.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No loop is present, or only the start node is processed without iterating further; the method returns without traversing the graph.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A loop is present but either the start node is not added to the queue before the loop begins, or the loop termination condition is incorrect (e.g., runs a fixed number of times rather than until the queue is empty).

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: The start node is added to the queue before the loop, and a while loop continues processing until the queue is empty.

rubric_points.name: FIFO dequeue: removing from the front
rubric_points.target: The current node is always removed from the front of the queue (index 0), ensuring breadth-first (level-by-level) traversal.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No dequeue operation is present, or nodes are removed from the back of the queue (LIFO), which produces depth-first rather than breadth-first traversal.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A remove operation is present but inconsistently applied (e.g., alternates between front and back), producing a traversal order that is neither pure BFS nor pure DFS.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: The current node is always removed from the front of the queue (remove(0) in Java, pop(0) in Python), ensuring nodes are processed in the order they were enqueued.

rubric_points.name: Visited tracking: check and mark
rubric_points.target: Each node is checked against the visited collection before being processed, and added to visited when processed, preventing duplicate entries and infinite loops.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No visited tracking is present; nodes can be added to the result multiple times or the algorithm loops infinitely on graphs with cycles.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A visited structure exists but is used incorrectly: for example, nodes are added to visited before being dequeued rather than when processed, or the visited check is present but the update is missing, allowing revisits.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Each dequeued node is checked against visited before processing; if not already visited it is added to visited and its neighbors are explored. Already-visited nodes are skipped.

rubric_points.name: Neighbour enqueue
rubric_points.target: All unvisited neighbours from graph[current] are iterated and added to the back of the queue.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Neighbours are not enqueued; graph[current] is not iterated, so no nodes beyond the start are ever discovered.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: graph[current] is iterated but all neighbours are enqueued regardless of visited status (causing duplicates), or only a subset of neighbours is enqueued (e.g., only the first).

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: All neighbours in graph[current] are iterated, and each unvisited neighbour is added to the back of the queue.

rubric_points.name: Overall correctness
rubric_points.target: The method returns all reachable nodes from start in correct BFS (level-by-level) order for all valid directed graphs.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Method returns an empty list or produces an incorrect traversal order for most inputs.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Returns correct nodes for simple linear or acyclic graphs but fails for graphs with cycles, multiple branches, or produces DFS order instead of BFS order.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Returns all reachable nodes from start in correct BFS order (level-by-level, neighbours in adjacency list order) for all valid directed graphs including those with cycles.

buckets.label: Internally consistent but incorrect final result
buckets.description: Logic is consistent with the student's own approach throughout, but the output order is incorrect due to a systematic error (e.g., consistently uses LIFO dequeue producing DFS order).
